title: Historian Query Saturation During Incident

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run, then normalised in Phase A (clause 'IEC 62443-4-2 / CR 7.2' → 'CR 7.2'). Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that IEC 62443 CR 7.2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.15.45: Historian Query Saturation During Incident.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.15.45' running on index=hist_queries, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=hist_queries sourcetype="hist:sql" earliest=-24h
| bin _time span=5m
| stats count as qps by _time, client_app, historian_cluster
| eventstats perc95(qps) as p95_global
| where qps > p95_global*3
| sort - qps
description: Tracks historian and OPC query rates to ensure analytical consumers do not starve operators during sustained attacks or mis-tuned reports.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for IEC 62443 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes hist_queries and sourcetypes hist:sql. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Separate reporting VLAN; (2) cap concurrent ODBC; (3) throttle BI tools; (4) burst alerts to ops. Indexes required: `index=hist_queries`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=hist:sql`.
visualization: Time chart, Table, Single value (saturated windows). Key fields: `client_app`, `historian_cluster`, `qps`.
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for tracks historian and opc query rates to ensure analytical consumers do not starve operators during sustained attacks or mis-tuned reports. This helps maintain steady compliance posture and reduces manual review effort.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Historian ODBC/API logs.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=hist_queries`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Separate reporting VLAN; (2) cap concurrent ODBC; (3) throttle BI tools; (4) burst alerts to ops.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=hist_queries sourcetype="hist:sql" earliest=-24h
| bin _time span=5m
| stats count as qps by _time, client_app, historian_cluster
| eventstats perc95(qps) as p95_global
| where qps > p95_global*3
| sort - qps
```

Understanding this SPL

**Historian Query Saturation During Incident (IEC 62443-4-2 / CR 7.2)** — Tracks historian and OPC query rates to ensure analytical consumers do not starve operators during sustained attacks or mis-tuned reports.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=hist_queries`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Historian ODBC/API logs. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: hist_queries; **sourcetype**: hist:sql. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=hist_queries, sourcetype="hist:sql", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time, client_app, historian_cluster** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• `eventstats` aggregates the pipeline (counts, distinct values, sums, percentiles, etc.) into fewer rows.
• Filters the current rows with `where qps > p95_global*3` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Time chart, Table, Single value (saturated windows).
